description: The supreme law of India establishing the framework for government and guaranteeing fundamental rights

sections.title: Equality before law
sections.text: The State shall not deny to any person equality before the law or the equal protection of the laws within the territory of India.
sections.simplified: Every person in India has the right to be treated equally by the law. No one — rich or poor, powerful or ordinary — is above the law.

sections.title: Prohibition of discrimination on grounds of religion, race, caste, sex or place of birth
sections.text: The State shall not discriminate against any citizen on grounds only of religion, race, caste, sex, place of birth or any of them.
sections.simplified: The government cannot discriminate against anyone based on their religion, race, caste, gender, or birthplace. However, the government can make special provisions for women, children, and backward classes.

sections.title: Equality of opportunity in matters of public employment
sections.text: There shall be equality of opportunity for all citizens in matters relating to employment or appointment to any office under the State.
sections.simplified: Every citizen has equal opportunity for government jobs. Reservations (affirmative action) for SCs, STs, OBCs, and EWS are constitutionally permitted.

sections.title: Abolition of Untouchability
sections.text: Untouchability is abolished and its practice in any form is forbidden. The enforcement of any disability arising out of Untouchability shall be an offence punishable in accordance with law.
sections.simplified: Untouchability is completely banned. Practicing untouchability in any form is a serious criminal offence. The Protection of Civil Rights Act 1955 provides punishments for this.

sections.title: Protection of certain rights regarding freedom of speech, etc.
sections.text: All citizens shall have the right to: (a) freedom of speech and expression; (b) assemble peaceably and without arms; (c) form associations or unions; (d) move freely throughout the territory of India; (e) reside and settle in any part of the territory of India; (f) practise any profession, or to carry on any occupation, trade or business.
sections.simplified: Every citizen has 6 fundamental freedoms: 1) Free speech, 2) Peaceful assembly, 3) Form associations/unions, 4) Travel freely in India, 5) Live anywhere in India, 6) Practice any profession/business. These rights have reasonable restrictions in the interest of public order, security, etc.

sections.title: Protection in respect of conviction for offences
sections.text: No person shall be convicted of any offence except for violation of the law in force at the time of the commission of the act. No person shall be prosecuted and punished for the same offence more than once. No person accused of any offence shall be compelled to be a witness against himself.
sections.simplified: Three protections for accused persons: 1) You can't be punished under a law that didn't exist when you did the act (no ex-post-facto law), 2) You can't be tried twice for the same offence (double jeopardy), 3) You can't be forced to testify against yourself (right against self-incrimination).

sections.title: Protection of life and personal liberty
sections.text: No person shall be deprived of his life or personal liberty except according to procedure established by law.
sections.simplified: The most important right: Right to Life and Personal Liberty. The Supreme Court has expanded this to include: right to livelihood, right to health, right to clean environment, right to privacy, right to dignity, right to education, right to shelter, and right to travel abroad.

sections.title: Right to education
sections.text: The State shall provide free and compulsory education to all children of the age of six to fourteen years in such manner as the State may, by law, determine.
sections.simplified: Every child between 6-14 years has the right to FREE and COMPULSORY education. This is enforced through the Right to Education (RTE) Act, 2009. Private schools must reserve 25% seats for economically weaker sections.

sections.title: Protection against arrest and detention
sections.text: No person who is arrested shall be detained in custody without being informed, as soon as may be, of the grounds for such arrest nor shall he be denied the right to consult, and to be defended by, a legal practitioner of his choice. Every person who is arrested and detained in custody shall be produced before the nearest magistrate within a period of twenty-four hours of such arrest.
sections.simplified: When arrested, you have the right to: 1) Know WHY you are arrested, 2) Consult a lawyer of your choice, 3) Be produced before a Magistrate within 24 hours. These rights cannot be denied.

sections.title: Prohibition of traffic in human beings and forced labour
sections.text: Traffic in human beings and begar and other similar forms of forced labour are prohibited and any contravention of this provision shall be an offence punishable in accordance with law.
sections.simplified: Human trafficking, bonded labour, and forced labour are completely banned and punishable by law. This includes child labour and debt bondage.

sections.title: Prohibition of employment of children in factories, etc.
sections.text: No child below the age of fourteen years shall be employed to work in any factory or mine or engaged in any other hazardous employment.
sections.simplified: Children under 14 cannot be employed in factories, mines, or any dangerous work. The Child Labour Act provides additional protections.

sections.title: Freedom of conscience and free profession, practice and propagation of religion
sections.text: Subject to public order, morality and health, all persons are equally entitled to freedom of conscience and the right freely to profess, practise and propagate religion.
sections.simplified: Every person has the right to follow any religion, practice their faith, and spread their religious beliefs. However, this right is subject to public order, morality, and health.

sections.title: Protection of interests of minorities
sections.text: Any section of the citizens residing in the territory of India or any part thereof having a distinct language, script or culture of its own shall have the right to conserve the same.
sections.simplified: Minorities (religious and linguistic) have the right to preserve their distinct language, script, and culture. They cannot be discriminated against in admission to educational institutions.

sections.title: Right of minorities to establish and administer educational institutions
sections.text: All minorities, whether based on religion or language, shall have the right to establish and administer educational institutions of their choice.
sections.simplified: Religious and linguistic minorities can set up and manage their own educational institutions. The government cannot discriminate against them in granting aid.

sections.title: Remedies for enforcement of rights (Right to Constitutional Remedies)
sections.text: The right to move the Supreme Court by appropriate proceedings for the enforcement of the rights conferred by this Part is guaranteed. The Supreme Court shall have power to issue directions or orders or writs, including writs in the nature of habeas corpus, mandamus, prohibition, quo warranto and certiorari.
sections.simplified: If any of your fundamental rights are violated, you can go DIRECTLY to the Supreme Court. Dr. Ambedkar called this the 'heart and soul of the Constitution'. The Supreme Court can issue writs to enforce your rights.

sections.title: State to secure a social order for the promotion of welfare of the people
sections.chapter: Part IV - Directive Principles of State Policy
sections.text: The State shall strive to promote the welfare of the people by securing and protecting as effectively as it may a social order in which justice, social, economic and political, shall inform all the institutions of the national life.
sections.simplified: The government should work to create a society where social, economic, and political justice is available to all citizens.

sections.title: Equal justice and free legal aid
sections.chapter: Part IV - Directive Principles of State Policy
sections.text: The State shall secure that the operation of the legal system promotes justice, on a basis of equal opportunity, and shall, in particular, provide free legal aid, by suitable legislation or schemes or in any other way, to ensure that opportunities for securing justice are not denied to any citizen by reason of economic or other disabilities.
sections.simplified: The government must provide FREE LEGAL AID to those who cannot afford a lawyer. This is implemented through the Legal Services Authority Act, 1987. Call NALSA helpline 15100 for free legal help.

sections.title: Uniform civil code for the citizens
sections.chapter: Part IV - Directive Principles of State Policy
sections.text: The State shall endeavour to secure for the citizens a uniform civil code throughout the territory of India.
sections.simplified: The Constitution directs the government to work towards a Uniform Civil Code (UCC) — a single set of personal laws for all citizens regardless of religion. Currently, different religions have different personal laws for marriage, divorce, inheritance, etc.

sections.title: Power of High Courts to issue certain writs
sections.text: Notwithstanding anything in Article 32, every High Court shall have powers to issue to any person or authority directions, orders or writs, including writs in the nature of habeas corpus, mandamus, prohibition, quo warranto and certiorari for the enforcement of any of the rights conferred by Part III and for any other purpose.
sections.simplified: High Courts can also issue writs to protect your fundamental rights and for any other purpose. You can approach your state's High Court instead of going directly to the Supreme Court.

sections.title: Definitions relating to Panchayats
sections.text: In this Part, Gram Sabha means a body consisting of persons registered in the electoral rolls relating to a village comprised within the area of Panchayat at the village level.
sections.simplified: Every village has a Gram Sabha (village assembly) consisting of all registered voters. Gram Panchayats are the lowest level of local self-governance where citizens can directly participate in decision-making.

sections.title: Fundamental Duties
sections.text: It shall be the duty of every citizen of India: (a) to abide by the Constitution and respect its ideals and institutions, the National Flag and the National Anthem; (b) to cherish and follow the noble ideals which inspired our national struggle for freedom; (c) to uphold and protect the sovereignty, unity and integrity of India; (d) to defend the country and render national service when called upon to do so; (e) to promote harmony and the spirit of common brotherhood amongst all the people of India; (f) to value and preserve the rich heritage of our composite culture; (g) to protect and improve the natural environment; (h) to develop the scientific temper, humanism and the spirit of inquiry and reform; (i) to safeguard public property and to abjure violence; (j) to strive towards excellence in all spheres of individual and collective activity.
sections.simplified: Citizens also have 10 fundamental duties: respect the Constitution and national symbols, promote harmony, protect the environment, develop scientific thinking, safeguard public property, and strive for excellence.
